heading: TONUS OCTAVUS
card_summary: This opening chapter of the Eighth Tone ("Tonus Octavus") announces the transition from Zorzi's structural analysis of the world-house — organized in enneads and intervals — to its living music: the inexplicable harmony that flows from the divine Breath (flatus) of the Creator. Zorzi marshals a dense catena of authorities — Orpheus (cited in Greek), the Pythagorean Dorylaus, Alexander of Miletus, Gregory of Nazianzus, Timaeus of Locri, Apuleius, and Augustine — all converging on the thesis that the world is God's instrument, its contrary forces unified in a single concordant sound. The chapter concludes with Augustine's observation that the harmony of all things surpasses the capacity of weak human minds to grasp.
essay:
## Overview

The opening chapter of the *Tonus Octavus* — "Deus omnia complet flatu, et vita suavissima" — marks a programmatic transition in *De Harmonia Mundi*. Having demonstrated in earlier tones that the cosmic house is arranged with its highest guest in the supreme concord of enneads and filled intervals, Zorzi now proposes to make audible the *melos* — the melody — of that house. This melody results, he declares, from the living and unfailing *flatus* (breath) of the Creator who fills all things, a harmony inexplicable in its nature (*inexplicabili harmonia*).

## The Divine Breath as Cosmic Music

Zorzi's key claim, announced in the title and opening lines, is that God fills all things with breath and the sweetest life (*flatu, et vita suavissima*). This is not merely metaphorical: the *flatus Opificis* is the actual ontological ground of cosmic harmony. The move is characteristic of Zorzi's Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic synthesis, where the divine emanation — analogous to the Kabbalistic concept of the divine breath animating creation — becomes the source of musical order.

> *omnia complentis inexplicabili harmonia resultat, de qua Orpheus: [Greek] tu totum coelum canora cithara temperas.*

<cite>p. 1094</cite>

The Greek citation from Orpheus — rendered in Latin as "tu totum coelum canora cithara temperas" ("you temper the whole heaven with your melodious lyre") — is drawn from the *Orphic Hymns* and establishes an ancient, pre-philosophical warrant for the world-as-instrument. Zorzi invokes Orpheus as the primordial theologian (*prisca theologia*) who first perceived this truth.

## The World as God's Instrument: A Chain of Witnesses

Zorzi builds his argument through a dense *catena* of authorities, each contributing a facet of the single thesis that the cosmos is a musical instrument played by the divine mind:

**Dorylaus the Pythagorean**: advances beyond Orpheus to state plainly that the world is God's instrument (*mundum esse organum Dei*). This lapidary Pythagorean formula crystallizes the cosmological-musical thesis.

**Alexander of Miletus and Gregory of Nazianzus**: cited together, they supply the key paradox — the world is praiseworthy for each individual species, but far more praiseworthy for the harmony of all things and the composition of the universe (*ex harmonia omnium, compageque universorum*). The crucial insight is that contraries — things mutually diverse — nonetheless converge in the same life and sound:

> *in quo summa est, et stupenda ex contrarietate in eadem vita, et sono concordia, dum inter se invicem diversa sunt, et cum singulis cuncta conveniunt, veluti instrumenti fistulae vel fibrae in eundem sonum congrediuntur.*

<cite>p. 1094</cite>

The simile of the organ pipes (*fistulae*) and strings (*fibrae*) converging into one sound is central: diversity of individual elements is not erased but elevated into concordance. This is Zorzi's philosophical signature — the *concordia discors* that runs throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*.

**Timaeus of Locri**: the Pythagorean source whom Plato drew upon testifies that the fabric of the world (*mundi fabrica*) endures in its elegant compactness (*concinnitas*) by an indissoluble and most excellent reason. The Platonic-Pythagorean cosmology of the *Timaeus* thus underwrites the stability of world-harmony.

**Apuleius**: the Platonist author is cited as one who emulates those same wise men, and contributes the image of the cosmic choir: one revolution of the world-sphere, one concord, one chorus of stars and other things from their diverse settings and risings (*unus concentus, atque unus stellarum, et aliarum rerum chorus ex diversis occasibus, ortibusque*). His analogy of the chorus-leader whose hymn leads a mixed crowd of men and women — voices grave and acute — into a single harmony is vivid:

> *sicut in choreis dux carminis hymno praecinit concinentium vulgus virorum, ac mulierum mixtis gravibus, et acutis clamoribus unam harmoniam resonant, sic divina mens mundanas varietates ad instar unius concentionis revelat.*

<cite>p. 1094–1095</cite>

The divine mind (*divina mens*) here plays the role of the chorus-master, revealing the world's varieties as a single performance. This is Zorzi's Neoplatonic theology in its most musical register.

**Augustine**: the chapter closes with a citation from Augustine's *De Ordine*, which observes that weak-minded humans (*homines imbecilli mente*) cannot fully embrace and contemplate the coaptation and concordance of all things — the *universarum rerum coaptationem atque concentum*. Augustine's epistemological humility frames the whole enterprise: we are reaching toward a harmony that exceeds our cognitive grasp.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

The *Tonus Octavus* opening explicitly situates itself as a sequel to the structural work of the prior tones: "Postquam mundanam hanc domum cum suo hospite summo concentu dispositam demonstravimus, et in enneadibus, et in repletis intervallis..." The enneadic structure and filled intervals are now to be heard rather than merely mapped. This shift from architecture to music — from spatial to temporal/sonic metaphors — signals a new phase in the treatise.

The dense chain of witnesses (Orpheus, Pythagoreans, Platonists, Church Fathers) is characteristic of Zorzi's method throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*: the *concordia* he seeks is not only cosmological but bibliographical, a harmony of ancient testimonies. The pairing of Gregory of Nazianzus with Alexander of Miletus, and Apuleius with the Timaeus, reflects Zorzi's commitment to showing that pagan philosophy and Christian theology speak with one voice on the fundamental structure of creation.

The technical vocabulary here — *flatus*, *concentus*, *concinnitas*, *coaptatio* — is drawn from both the Latin musical-philosophical tradition and the Stoic-Neoplatonic vocabulary of divine immanence, filtered through Zorzi's Kabbalistic reading of the divine breath animating creation.
